The table following table describes Oracle data types, but is only intended as a guide and is not a complete description of Oracle storage formats. If you are in any doubt about the exact requirements of your environment, see your Oracle documentation.

Table 2. Oracle Storage Formats
Storage Format Description
CHAR Used to store fixed-length character strings. You may specify up to a maximum of 255 bytes for a Char column (the default length is one byte). When storing Char columns, Oracle pads them out with blanks to their maximum length. When it compares two Char column values, Oracle uses blank-padded comparison semantics.

The character set used by the database is defined at the time the database is created.
VARCHAR2 Used to store variable-length character strings. You may specify up to a maximum of 4000 bytes for a Varchar2 column (the default length is one byte). Oracle uses variable-length storage techniques when it stores values in Varchar2 columns. When it compares two Varchar2 column values, Oracle uses non-padded comparison semantics.
NCHAR A fixed length data type that can be used to store NLS or multibyte character set data. The maximum size is 2000 bytes per row or 2000 characters.
NVARCHAR2 Used to store variable-length NLS or multibyte character set data.
NUMBER Used for fixed and floating point numbers. The values you can store range from -9.99...* 10 125 through 9.99...* 10 125 , and can have a maximum length of 38 significant digits.

Uniface allows you to specify non-default decimal characters by setting the Oracle parameter NLS_NUMERIC_CHARACTERS (for example, if you want to use a character other than the period (.) to represent the decimal point). This does not change the way Uniface interprets and presents numeric values, except in the sql ProcScript command, the SQL Workbench and the read where ProcScript command.
DATE Used to store point-in-time values, which consist of both a date and a time. The values which can be stored in a Date field range from Jan 1, 4712 BC 00.00.00 through Dec 31, 4712 AD 23.59.59.

If no time is available with a value to be stored in the Date storage format, Uniface adds the default time of 00:00:00. If time is recorded without date information, Uniface adds the NULL date of 01011900 (1 January, 1900). Arithmetic functions with Date are possible.

Uniface allows you to specify non-default date formats by setting Oracle parameters such as NLS_DATE_FORMAT. This does not change the way Uniface interprets and presents Date values, except in the sql ProcScript command, the SQL Workbench and the read where ProcScript command.
LONG Used to store variable-length character data to a maximum length of two gigabytes. The following restrictions apply when using the Long storage format:
  • An Oracle table may contain only one Long or Long Raw column.
  • Stored procedure arguments cannot be declared using the Long storage format.
  • Long and Long Raw columns cannot appear in integrity constraints (except for NOT NULL).
  • Long columns cannot be indexed.
  • Long columns cannot appear in the where clause, the order by clause, nor in SQL functions, expressions and conditions.
  • Segmented read is possible with Long columns, but segmented write is not.
  • When you use the sql ProcScript statement or the SQL Workbench to retrieve Long data, it is truncated at 42 bytes. To avoid this truncation, use a Uniface entity that maps to the table and do a normal retrieve of the entity.

Uniface uses the Long storage format in the following circumstances:

  • When a field is defined in Uniface as having data type S with packing code C, VC, U, or VU, and that field exceeds the maximum length of the Varchar2 storage format, Uniface uses the Long storage format. Uniface cannot store or retrieve values which exceed the length defined for the field in the application model. This restriction is not enforced by Oracle, however, as Long columns do not have a declared maximum length.
  • For variable-length fields. If you define a variable-length field in Uniface, it is stored in a Long or Long Raw column of an overflow table (this is according to Uniface's own variable-length techniques).

To access a Long column in an existing database, you must use the S data type and the VC* packing code.
RAW Used to store binary data which Oracle does not convert or interpret. You may specify a length up to a maximum of 255 bytes for a Raw column.
LONG RAW Used to store large amounts of binary data which Oracle does not convert or interpret. You can store up to 2 gigabytes (the theoretical limit) of data in a Long Raw column. The ways in which Uniface uses the Long Raw storage format, and the limitations which apply to its use are the same as those described for the Long storage format.

To access a Long Raw column in an existing database, you must use the R data type and the VR* packing code.
CLOB and BLOB Used to store CLOB and BLOB data, respectively, where Uniface fields are defined with data type S and packing code SC. When you define fields with the segmented packing code SC, Uniface can store data to a maximum length of 4 gigabytes. These fields are treated like any other except that they may not be used in a condition, either explicitly, through a u_where or where ProcScript, or implicitly, by entering data in a CLOB or BLOB field before requesting a retrieve.
NCLOB (National Character Large Object) is an Oracle data type that can hold up to 4 GB of character data. It is similar to a CLOB, but characters are stored in a NLS or multibyte national character set.
ROWID Physical addresses of rows in the database. Uniface uses ROWIDs because they are the fastest way to access individual rows. This speed of access becomes particularly important when Uniface must address a row several times in quick succession (for example, the first time to select a row with its ROWID, the second time to lock that row and the third time to update that row).

Uniface uses ROWIDs automatically. It is neither necessary or possible to define the Rowid pseudo column of a table in the application model.

Entities that are accessed through Oracle Gateway products use ROWIDs that vary from zero through 255 bytes. Uniface cannot currently handle variable-length ROWIDs but the disable rowid connector option can be set so that the connector can handle these entities.

Connector Options Affecting Data Storage

When set, the following connector options affect data storage:

  • map fixed length to variable—all fields that would normally be mapped to the Char storage format are instead mapped to the Varchar2 storage format.
  • disable segmented fields—Uniface transparently maps fields with segmented packing codes into the variable portion of the record, using Uniface's own variable-length techniques.
  • varchar2_4k—fields from C2001 through C4000 are mapped to Varchar2 instead of to Clob, and fields with packing code W2001 through W4000 are mapped to NVarchar2 instead of NClob.
Related Links
  • Storage Formats

    DBMS vendors use the term data type to describe the way data is handled and stored on disk, but this invariably means the way the data is actually stored. In Uniface documentation, the term storage format is therefore used to refer to DBMS data types . A Uniface data type is specified for each field in a modeled entity; a DBMS storage format is internal to the DBMS.

  • Packing Codes

    Packing codes are mappings between Uniface data types and DBMS data types (storage formats). When writing a field, Uniface passes the data and a valid packing code to the DBMS connector, which uses the packing code to determine the appropriate storage format for storing the data in the target DBMS. Packing codes also determine how data is interfaced with 3GL routines.
